1:
On this verse the cloud of witnesses is, at root, unanimous: across the Greek and Latin fathers, the Reformers, and the modern grammatical commentators alike, John 1:1 is read as the church's charter for the eternal deity of Christ — the Word is eternal (not made), personal and distinct from the Father ("with God"), and himself fully divine ("was God"). No school dissents from this; their differences are of emphasis and approach, not of doctrine.

The grammatical-historical commentators are the most active here, and supply the verse's sharpest exegesis. With one voice they hear the opening as a deliberate echo of Genesis 1:1 — a Jew would name Genesis itself by its first word, Bĕrēshîth — so that the Word by whom creation was made now brings about redemption. They press the tenses: John writes the timeless "was" (ēn), not "became," carrying thought (as Robertson puts it) beyond the beginning of creation into eternity. And they handle the grammar a later age would seize on: "the Word was God" lacks the article before God not to weaken it to "a god" but to mark it as a predicate of nature — the Word is fully divine, while remaining the distinct One who was "with God."

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) read doctrinally: this is the essential, personal Word — not the written word — whose eternal divinity grounds the very necessity of redemption through the Son. The Latin fathers, with Augustine, strike a pastoral and epistemic note: "the natural man perceiveth not the things of the Spirit," so the height of the verse is reached by faith, not cleverness — Augustine recalls the Platonist who said these words deserved to be written in letters of gold. The Antiochene Chrysostom frames our very approach: we need not trace John's family or schooling, "since it is not he, but God by him, that speaks to mankind."

Where the witnesses press differently is the sense of "beginning." Most (Lightfoot, Wesley, and the expositors) anchor it in the beginning of creation in Genesis; the grammarians and the Alexandrian Origen — here the outlier — reach past creation: Origen catalogues the word's many scriptural senses and finds Christ to be himself the Beginning as Wisdom. But this is a deepening, not a disagreement: every school confesses the same eternal, divine, personal Word who is the doorway to all the Gospel will unfold.
2:
On the brief recapitulation of v.2 the witnesses are wholly united: this is no idle repetition but a deliberate safeguard, gathering up the three assertions of v.1 — pre-existence, distinct personality, and deity — into one guarded summary, so that the eternal union of the Word with God can never be mistaken for something begun in time.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work. Barnes observes that the first verse had said the Word existed before creation and was "with God," but had not said that this union itself "existed in the beginning"; the second verse now "guards the doctrine, and prevents the possibility of a mistake," asserting a union "not one which was commenced in time." Ellicott calls it "a summary in one clause of the three assertions" — the Word who was God existed before any act of creation and in that existence was a person distinct from God — yet, viewed "from this higher point of view, the steps below us are more clearly seen." Robertson hears in the repeated "this one" (houtos) John's characteristic style, linking two ideas already stated apart: "in the beginning he was with God," and "afterwards in time he came to be with man."

The Reformed voices press the same point doctrinally. Calvin says the Evangelist "condenses the two preceding clauses into a brief summary," so that it may be understood the Word "was before all time"; Gill adds that the repetition shows "the importance of the truths before delivered," proving "not only his eternal existence, but his eternal existence with the Father, and also his eternal deity." The Latin Hilary, the one patristic voice here, turns it apologetically: the strangeness of "the Word was God" had disturbed him, since the prophets declared God to be one; but "to quiet my apprehensions, the fisherman reveals the scheme of this so great mystery," referring all to the one unbegotten God "without dishonour, without obliterating the Person." The Wesleyan Wesley simply notes that the verse "repeats and contracts into one the three points mentioned before." There is no debate here, only a shared resolve that the doctrine of v.1 be heard exactly, with no opening for a temporal or tritheistic misreading.
3:
On v.3 the cloud of witnesses is unanimous in substance: all things came into being through the Word, and this universal creatorship proves his true deity. The Genesis echo of v.1 is sustained — the Word by whom Genesis 1:1 was wrought is the same who now brings redemption.

The grammatical-historical commentators are most active. They mark the decisive verb: "all things" (panta) "came into being" (egeneto, the constative aorist of creation) over against the timeless "was" (ēn) of the Word himself (Robertson), who notes that the philosophical phrase for the universe, "the all things," recurs in 1 Corinthians 8:6 and Romans 11:36. "All things" means the entire universe, with nothing excepted, and they gather the parallels in Colossians 1:16, Hebrews 1:2, and Revelation 4:11 (Barnes, Ellicott); the added clause "without him was not any thing made" is set there precisely to bar any exception.

The Reformed voices read it as proof of deity drawn from works. Calvin says the Evangelist, "having affirmed that the Word is God... now proves his Divinity from his works," and makes it practical: "the mere name of God attributed to Christ will affect us little, if our faith do not feel it to be such by experience"; he notes that what Scripture sometimes ascribes generally to God it here ascribes strictly to the person of the Son. Gill guards against limiting "all things" to a supposed new creation of a few souls — "it is false that all were converted" — insisting the words mean the original making of the universe.

The Wesleyan expositors sharpen the polemic. Clarke insists creation means "causing that to exist that had no previous being," a work "which can be effected only by omnipotence," so that to say Christ created by a delegated power is "absurd, because the thing is impossible" — therefore "Christ and the Father are One." Wesley charts the verses as a movement from the state before creation, to the creation, to the time of innocence and of corruption. The Greek fathers press the universality from their own angle: the Antiochene Chrysostom takes "without him nothing was made" to include even what is "cognizable... by the understanding," lest the verse be confined to the visible creation of Moses; the Alexandrian Origen weighs the many senses of "beginning," dismissing the merely transitional ones; and Cyril of Jerusalem rebukes the curiosity that would scrutinise the thrones and dominions which are themselves Christ's workmanship (cf. Colossians 1:16). No school dissents; their differences only emphasise one confession — the Word is Creator, and therefore God.
4:
In v.4 the witnesses agree that the Word is the very fountain of life, and that this life is also "the light of men"; their differences lie in how widely to take "life" and where the verse turns from creation to redemption.

The grammatical-historical reading takes "life" in its widest sense. Robertson calls the Logos "the power that creates and sustains life in the universe," comparing the upholding "by the word of his power" of Hebrews 1:3 and the claim of John 11:25; Barnes recalls that God "breathed into man the breath of life" in Genesis 2:7, and that this attribute of being the source of life is here ascribed to Christ — the agent "by which the vegetable world became animated; by which brutes live," and by which the intellectual life of man exists. Ellicott takes "life" with "no limitation... in its widest sense." The Reformed Calvin makes this explicit: the verse moves from the bare act of creation to its continual preservation — "not merely... a sudden exercise of his power, which soon passed away, but... the steady and regular order of nature" — extending even to "inanimate creatures, which live after their own manner"; Gill adds that this life is in the Word "originally, and independently, and from all eternity."

A second emphasis, carried by the Greek and Latin witnesses, hears "the light of men" as intellectual and spiritual light. Theophylact, citing Augustine, makes it the light of the rational soul: "Life of itself gives illumination to men, but to cattle not, for they have not rational souls... whereas man, being made in the image of God, has a rational soul, by which he can discern wisdom." Lightfoot finds here the pivot of the whole prologue — the Word who "had given to all creatures their first being" now "restored life to man when he lay dead in trespasses and sins."

The Wesleyan expositors stand close to this. Wesley calls the Word "the fountain of wisdom, holiness, and happiness, to man in his original state," while Clarke draws a careful line: the mere animal "life or animal principle in the human being was not the light of men" — "not that light which could guide them to heaven." So the schools complement rather than collide: the one Word is both the life of all that lives and the saving light of rational men.
5:
On v.5 all the witnesses agree that the light is the Word and the darkness is fallen, ignorant humanity, and that the light "keeps on shining" (Robertson, noting the linear present phainei and the late word skotia for the darkness of sin). Where they divide is over the single word translated "comprehended" (katelaben).

One side, led by the Latin Augustine and joined by the Wesleyan Wesley, reads it as failure to perceive or receive. Augustine gives the image of the blind man in the sun: "the sun is present to him, but he is absent from the sun. In like manner every fool is blind, and wisdom is present to him; but, though present... is absent from him." Wesley puts it tersely — the light "shines even on fallen man; but the darkness, dark sinful man, perceiveth it not." The other side, carried by several grammatical-historical voices, reads katelaben as "overcame" or "suppressed": Clarke (following Wakefield) renders it "prevented it not, hindered it not," noting that "even in the midst of that darkness... this light of Divine wisdom was not totally eclipsed"; Lightfoot agrees that the cloudy types and shadows of the law could not so cloud the light "but it would break out." Both senses confess the same truth from opposite angles — the light is neither understood nor extinguished by the dark.

The Reformed Gill and Calvin supply the doctrinal ground. Gill spreads out the darkness as ignorance "about the nature and perfections of God... about sin... about Christ... about the Spirit... and about the Scriptures," and Calvin insists that men "by their own guidance, are led only to vanity and lies," so the light shines into a darkness of their own making, while still leaving them "without excuse." The Alexandrian Origen, the subtlest here, distinguishes two lights: the light that "shines in darkness and is not overtaken by it" is not identical with the light "in which there is no darkness at all" of 1 John 1:5; the former "comes upon" a darkness it must penetrate. Ellicott reads the whole as the long history of the light pressing into the moral chaos — as God once said, "Let there be light" — never quenched, first under the shadows of the law and then in the flesh.
6:
With v.6 the prologue turns from the eternal Word to his herald, and the witnesses note the deliberate change of register. The grammatical-historical commentators fix on the verb: John "came into being" (egeneto, the same word as in v.3), in pointed contrast to the Word who simply "was" (Ellicott, who renders it "there appeared a man"). Robertson marks the perfect participle "having been sent" (apestalmenos) and the parenthetic "name to him, John"; "a man" is emphatically opposed to "the Word" — the Word was God; the man "was sent from God" (para theou, from God's side). Barnes observes that the evangelist is at pains to show John "was not the Messiah," yet was "divinely commissioned" on "an important errand to men."

The Latin Augustine presses why so great a witness was needed: Christ "came to us in the form of man, but man in such sense as that the Godhead was concealed within Him. And therefore there was sent before a great man, to declare by his witness that He was more than man" — and "how could he declare the truth concerning God, unless he were sent from God?" The Antiochene Chrysostom draws the practical lesson: now that we hear John "was sent from God," we must "not for the future imagine that any of the words spoken by him are mere man's words; for all that he utters is not his own, but is of Him who sent him" — wherefore he is called a "messenger."

The Reformed Gill and Calvin dwell on the divine arrangement. Calvin says God "sent this witness before his Christ, that they might more willingly receive the salvation offered by him"; Gill recalls John's extraordinary birth of a barren woman and his uncommon ministry. The Puritan Henry notes the general truth first asserted — "he was a man sent of God" — and that the name John signifies "gracious." The Alexandrian Origen asks the "whence and whither" of his sending: plainly "to Israel" by the Jordan, and, "according to the deeper sense," into the world. Across the schools the verdict is one: the forerunner's whole greatness lies in being sent.
7:
The witnesses read v.7 as the statement of the Baptist's entire purpose: he came "for a witness," to testify of the Light, "that all men through him" — through John's testimony — "might believe."

The grammatical-historical commentators stress that "witness" (marturia) is a characteristic word of this Gospel; Robertson calls it "the purpose of the Baptist's ministry," and notes the final clause "that he might bear witness" added to make the aim clearer. Ellicott compares the evangelist's own stated purpose at the close of John 20 — testimony exists for conviction — and Barnes explains that John came "to prepare the minds of the people to receive him... to lead them by repentance to God; and to point out the Messiah to Israel when he came." The Reformed Calvin and Gill set the same purpose within the frame of the church: John's calling was "that he might prepare a Church for Christ," and "by inviting all to Christ, he shows plainly enough that he did not come on his own account."

The Alexandrian Origen turns the verse against the "dissenters from the Church's doctrine" who, professing Christ, deny that his coming was foretold by the prophets and so "endeavour to get rid of the testimonies of the prophets," pretending the Son "has no need of witnesses"; the verse shows that the Son does indeed receive and appoint witness. The Wesleyan reading interweaves John's testimony with the evangelist's. Clarke says John "testified that Jesus was the true light... and the Lamb or sacrifice of God, which was to bear away the sin of the world" (cf. John 1:29), inviting men to believe "that they might receive the baptism of the Holy Ghost" (John 1:32-34); Wesley observes that what the other evangelists call "the Gospel," John, "with the most strong and tender affection," habitually terms "the testimony," intimating "the certain knowledge of the relator." There is no dissent — only one confession that the forerunner exists for the sake of faith in Another. Wesley observes that the evangelist "interweaves his own testimony with that of John," explaining "the office of the Baptist," so that herald and apostle bear one witness.
8:
On v.8 the witnesses agree that the emphatic correction guards the glory of Christ. The grammatical-historical reading is precise. Robertson notes that John "was a light" — as believers are called the light of the world in Matthew 5:14, and as Jesus himself names the Baptist a burning and shining lamp in John 5:35 — "but not the light" (to phōs); and that the verb of motion is to be supplied from v.7, "but came." Ellicott observes that "it is necessary to repeat the statement of John's position and work in an emphatic form," because John's appearance was itself an event: "now first for 400 years a great teacher had appeared in Israel," whose "bold message, like the cry of another Elias," drew the masses, so that "from Pharisee and Sadducee, from publican and soldier," men asked whether he might be the Christ. Barnes simply anchors the point with the witness of Acts 19:4.

The Reformed Gill and Calvin draw out why the warning was needed. Gill grants that John had "great light in him" — he "knew that the Messiah was ready to come, and declared it," and at the baptism "knew him personally, and signified him to others"; he had "great light into the person and work of Christ, and into the way of salvation by him," and into "faith in Christ, and... evangelical repentance" — yet remained only the forerunner, "the phosphorus of the Gospel day." Calvin gives the memorable image: the caution is set down "lest his excessive brightness might obscure the glory of Christ," for some "gazed so eagerly upon him that they neglected Christ; just as if a person, enraptured with beholding the dawning of the day, would not deign to turn his eyes towards the sun" — and he notes that all the godly are indeed called light (Ephesians 5:8), but only by a borrowed brightness. The voices here are few and the comment brief, but their verdict is single: the lamp must not be mistaken for the dawn it announces. Robertson notes that, in Johannine fashion, the final clause of v.7 — "that all... might believe" — is here repeated, binding the two verses into one thought.
9:
On v.9 the witnesses unite in confessing Christ as "the true Light," and divide over two finer questions: the construction of "that cometh into the world," and the sense in which he "lighteth every man."

On "true," the schools converge: it means not "true as opposed to false" but the genuine, archetypal Light. The Reformed Calvin states it best — Christ is "the Light, shining from itself and by itself, and enlightening the whole world by its radiance," while "whatever is luminous in heaven and in earth borrows its splendour from some other object." The grammatical-historical voices agree: Ellicott says "true" means "answering to the perfect ideal," not opposed to false; Barnes contrasts a "true beacon" that guides into port with a "false light" that lures ships "to quicksands or rocks"; "Christ does not lead astray." The Latin Augustine adds that "man enlightened is called light, but the true Light is that" which enlightens.

Two debates remain. First, the syntax: Lightfoot reads "every man that cometh into the world" as a Hebrew idiom for all who are born — "all the men that are in the world" — citing the rabbinic refrain ("Doth not the sun rise upon all that come into the world?"), so that Christ, "shining forth in the light of the gospel, is a light that lightens all the world." Ellicott and Robertson prefer to attach the participle to the Light itself: "the true Light... was coming into the world," manifesting itself while men "mistook the lamp for the light." Second, the scope of the lighting: the Wesleyan reading takes it of a universal natural illumination — Wesley's "natural conscience, pointing out at least the general lines of good and evil," and Clarke's "human intellect is a ray from his brightness... reason itself springs from this Logos." The grammatical and Reformed voices keep the stress on the saving light now manifested. The Antiochene Chrysostom, building his exposition "little by little" like a careful mason, carries the thought back past "the recent coming of the witness" to that existence "which is beyond all beginning." All confess one genuine Light; they differ only on its grammar and the breadth of its shining.
10:
v.10 sets a tragic paradox before the witnesses — the world's Maker was in the world, "and the world knew him not" — and they divide chiefly over whether "he was in the world" speaks of the pre-incarnate, providential presence or of the Incarnation.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices lean to the former. Robertson reads the imperfect "was" as "continuous existence in the universe before the Incarnation," as in vv.1–2, and calls "knew him not" a "negative aorist," a world that "refused or failed to recognize him." The Wesleyan Clarke is most expansive: Christ "was in the world from its very commencement — he governed the universe — regulated his Church — spake by his prophets — and often, as the angel or messenger of Jehovah, appeared to them, and to the patriarchs"; men "love the world, and this love hinders them from knowing him who made it." Gill takes "he was in the world" of the Word filling and upholding the world "by his essence" and "by his providence," and Calvin makes the verse an indictment of ingratitude — men drew daily from "a running stream" yet "never thought of the fountain from which that stream flows," a stupidity reaching "to every age of the world." Barnes, by contrast, refers "he was in the world" to the Incarnation itself, when the Word "became incarnate" and "dwelt among men."

On "the world knew him not," the Latin and Greek fathers distinguish the senses of "world." Augustine notes that the same word names both the creation "made by Him" and "the lovers of the world... of whom he says, the world knew Him not." Theophylact uses the verse to overthrow at once "the insane notion of the Manichaean, who says that the world is the work of a malignant creature, and the opinion of the Arian, that the Son of God is a creature." Origen offers his image of the spoken word that vanishes when the speaker ceases — were "the Heavenly Father" to "cease to speak His Word," the created universe would cease to be. Cyril of Jerusalem gathers Colossians 1:16 to confess Christ "the Maker... not only of the things which are seen, but also of the things which are not seen." The schools meet in the wonder that the Light by which all things were made shone unrecognised in his own creation.
11:
On v.11 the witnesses agree that the verse moves to the historic advent ("he came," over against "he was" of v.10) and turns on the two forms of "his own." The grammatical-historical commentators make the grammar decisive: "his own" is first neuter (ta idia, "his own things," his own home — the very idiom of John 19:27, and compare the margin of John 16:32 and Acts 21:6), the world he had made; then masculine (hoi idioi, "his own people," even "his own intimates" or family, as in John 13:1), who "received him not" (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). Barnes glosses the first "his own" as "his own land or country," the birth-place and "chosen land where God delighted to dwell" (cf. Isaiah 5:1-7; Psalm 147:19-20).

The Reformed Gill and Calvin set the rejection against election: Israel had been "separated to God from the other nations to be His own heritage," his own by creation and by choice, and yet, "when the Son of God was manifested in flesh to the Jews," he was not received — here, says Calvin, "is displayed the absolutely desperate wickedness and malice of men," though he allows that interpreters have explained "his own" both of the whole world and of Judea. The Wesleyan Wesley localises it sharply — Christ came "to his own country, city, temple," and "his own people received him not."

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads "the world knew him not" of the old dispensation and "he came to his own" of the time of his preaching, and marvels that he who "fills all things, and is every where present" should "come" at all — he came "not for His own good, but for the good of others," purely "out of condescension to us." Cyril of Jerusalem hears the fulfilment of Malachi 3:1: "the Lord shall come to His own temple," where "ye stoned Him." The one debate is the breadth of "his own" — the whole world he owns by creation, or Judea and Israel his peculiar heritage — but every school confesses the same grief: the Maker, come home, was turned away.
12:
Against the rejection of v.11, v.12 lifts up the believing remnant, and the witnesses agree on its heart: to "as many as received him" — that is, who "believe on his name" — he gave the right to "become the sons of God."

The grammatical-historical voices correct the rendering "power": the word (exousia) is better "right" or "privilege" (the margin), and Barnes, Clarke, Lightfoot, and Robertson agree. Barnes lists why believers are called sons of God — by adoption, by likeness of character, by being "objects of his peculiar love." Clarke calls it "the greatest privilege which the Divine Being can confer on this side eternity," a sonship "purchased" by Christ's "blood" and confirmed by "the fullest promises of God." Lightfoot notes that "Israel was once the son and the first-born" (Exodus 4:22), "but now the adoption of sons to God was open and free to all nations." Robertson observes the Johannine anacoluthon (the dative "to them" resuming the relative clause), an idiom he and others trace to an Aramaic background.

The Reformed Calvin and Gill stress the universal "as many as," which Calvin says "contains an implied contrast" with the "blind vaunting" of the Jews who imagined the privilege theirs by descent; faith, not blood, is the receiving — and Gill defines that receiving as taking Christ "as the word, and Son of God, as the Messiah, Saviour, and Redeemer."

The Wesleyan Wesley makes it immediate and inclusive — "Jews or Gentiles... the moment they believe, they are sons; and because they are sons, God sendeth forth the Spirit of his Son into their hearts, crying, Abba, Father" (cf. Romans 8:15). The catechetical Cyril of Jerusalem guards the difference between this and Christ's own sonship: believers "become sons, but by adoption of grace, as it is written," whereas the Son is Son "by nature, a true Son," and "began not His being" when he was born. So the schools confess one gospel of adoption — a right freely given, received by faith, and open to all who believe. Lightfoot glosses the verb plainly: "He empowered them," giving "the privilege, the liberty, the dignity, of being called and becoming the sons of God."
13:
v.13 explains the sonship of v.12: it is by a birth "not of blood... nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of man, but of God." The witnesses are agreed that this is regeneration — a spiritual begetting, not natural descent, and least of all descent from Abraham.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the plural "bloods" (ex haimatōn). Lightfoot connects it with Jewish notions of being made sons of God by "the blood of circumcision" and the blood of the passover "mingled" in Egypt, asking whether the evangelist opposes regeneration to natural generation or to "those ways by which the Jews fancied men were made the sons of God." Barnes takes "born" as the great change of John 3:3 (cf. 1 John 2:29) — a new birth that "introduces us to life," excluding the idea that men become God's children "because they were descended from pious parents." The Reformed Gill names it plainly: regeneration — being "quickened by the Spirit," having "Christ formed" in one, "a partaking of the divine nature," made "new creatures" — which "is the evidence of their being the sons of God."

The Wesleyan reading presses the threefold negation. Clarke notes that the plural "bloods" marks dignity in Hebrew idiom — "the union of father and mother, or... a distinguished or illustrious ancestry" — and that "having Abraham and Sarah for their parents would not entitle them" to the new covenant. Wesley spells out the three: "not of blood — not by descent from Abraham; nor by the will of the flesh — by natural generation; nor by the will of man — adopting them; but of God — by his Spirit." The catechetical Cyril of Jerusalem again sets this adoption of grace beside the Son's natural sonship. The notable outlier is Robertson, who records the ancient variant — in the Old Latin, Irenaeus, and Tertullian — reading the singular "who was born," referring the clause to Christ himself and so teaching the Virgin Birth, though he holds the plural, of believers, to be the true text. The schools confess one new birth: sons of God made so by God alone.
14:
If verse 1 guards the Word's eternal deity, the witnesses hear verse 14 as its astonishing counterpart, and again they are unanimous on the substance: the same eternal Word truly "became flesh" — took full and real humanity — without ceasing to be God. The schools converge especially against any docetic dilution: this is a real incarnation, not an appearance.

The grammatical-historical commentators again do the close work. They mark the decisive shift of verb — John now writes egeneto ("became"), the historic event of the Incarnation, over against the timeless ēn of verse 1 (Robertson) — and note that "flesh" stands without the article, so the clause can never be reversed into flesh dissolving the Word. On the word "flesh" they (with Barnes) settle its sense as full human nature, the whole man; Ellicott draws the line explicitly against the Docetists who denied a real body: "Man came to be a son of God, because the Son of God became man."

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) add Trinitarian precision and continuity: it was the Son alone — not the Father or the Spirit — who was clothed with our flesh and shown openly to the world; "that very person who was in the beginning." The Antiochene Chrysostom presses the logic of grace: we are made sons of God only because the Word was made flesh, and we could never have seen his glory had he not, by union with our humanity, become visible to us.

On "we beheld his glory," the Wesleyan reading (Wesley) is the most concrete: the "we" are the apostles — Peter, James, and John, who saw the glory "as of the only begotten" — and Wesley canvasses the senses of "flesh" (corrupt nature, the body, or, here, the whole man) before settling, with the consensus, on full humanity. He turns the verse Godward: against our native condition as "liars and children of wrath," grace and truth come in him. There is no real dissent here, only complementary angles on one confession: the eternal Word truly took our whole humanity, tabernacled among us, and let his divine glory be seen — that we might receive of his fulness, grace upon grace.
15:
v.15 resumes the Baptist's testimony, and the witnesses fasten on its paradox: "He that cometh after me is preferred before me: for he was before me." The grammatical-historical commentators distinguish the senses. Barnes notes that most critics relate "preferred before me" to dignity rather than time — "is superior to me" — even though, in the flesh, Jesus was "born after" John; Ellicott and Robertson agree the three phrases mark order in time ("after me"), rank ("preferred before me"), and eternal pre-existence ("he was before me"). Robertson points to the vivid verbs: the historic present "beareth witness" (marturei) and the second perfect "crieth" (kekragen), a cry that abides and is repeated three times within these verses.

The Reformed Gill and Calvin hear in the present tense a continuing testimony: Calvin says the verb is used "in the present tense" to denote "a continued act," as "if the voice of John were continually resounding in the ears of men," a doctrine that "must be continually in force." The medieval Latin Alcuin says the witness "plainly declared the excellence of His Human Nature and the Eternity of His Godhead," reasoning that if the Jews' law "admits the testimony of two men who may be deceived," how much more the testimony of the Father and the Son. The Antiochene Chrysostom guards its disinterestedness: John "had never seen Him before, nor tarried with Him, nor partook of His table," so he witnessed not "out of gratitude."

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the distinction into a maxim — "He is preferred before me, in his office: for he was before me, in his nature" — adding that John "knew him first at his baptism." The Puritan Henry observes that, just as "the Old-Testament prophets cried aloud, to show people their sins," so "this New-Testament prophet cried aloud, to show people their Saviour." Cyril of Jerusalem ties the cry to the promise that the Coming One "shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire." The witnesses agree throughout: the forerunner's loud, public testimony confesses the One who follows him in time but precedes him in dignity and in eternal being.
16:
On v.16 the witnesses agree on the substance — that from Christ's "fulness" all believers receive, and that grace comes in unceasing succession — while differing over the speaker and the precise phrase "grace for grace."

There is a real debate over whose words these are. The Alexandrian Origen insists the verse continues the Baptist's testimony, holding it "inadmissible" that "on a sudden... the discourse of the Baptist should be interrupted by a speech of the disciple's," and finding "a very obvious connection" with "he was before me." The Antiochene Chrysostom likewise reads it as John's further reason for the One's priority. The grammatical-historical voices demur: Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson hold these to be the evangelist's words, "closely connected" with "full of grace and truth" in v.14, as the phrase "all we" shows. On the substance the schools agree. Robertson notes that "fulness" (plērōma) — the only instance of the word in John — is the Pauline term for "all the attributes of God summed up in Christ" (Colossians 1:19; Colossians 2:9; Ephesians 1:23). Barnes adds that the disciples "derived from his abundant truth and mercy grace to understand the plan of salvation, to preach the gospel, to live lives of holiness."

On "grace for grace" (charin anti charitos), the Wesleyan Wesley reads it as accumulation — "one blessing upon another, immeasurable grace and love" — while Lightfoot gives it a missionary turn: believers received "grace towards the propagation of grace," even "the grace of apostleship, that we might dispense and propagate the grace of the gospel towards others." The Reformed Calvin presses the doctrinal weight: Christ's office "contains within itself an abundance of all blessings, so that no part of salvation must be sought anywhere else"; the fountain of life and righteousness, "hidden and inaccessible" in God, is opened in Christ, "ready to flow to us, provided that we open up a channel by faith." Gill distinguishes the "incommunicable" fulness of the Godhead from the "dispensatory" fulness of grace and truth from which the church draws. Across the schools the verse confesses one inexhaustible Christ, from whom grace is drawn upon grace.
17:
v.17 draws the great contrast — "the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ" — and the witnesses agree it sets the preparatory below the fulfilment without setting the two at enmity.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the verbs and prepositions. Robertson notes that the law "was given" (edothē) "through Moses" (dia), "the intermediate agent of God," while grace and truth "came" (egeneto, "the historical event, the beginning of Christianity") "through Jesus Christ"; and that here, for the first time in John, the full name "Jesus Christ" appears, so that the evangelist "identifies the Pre-incarnate Logos with Jesus of Nazareth, the Messiah." Barnes adds that the law "worketh wrath" (Romans 4:15) and "was attended with many burdensome rites" (Acts 15:10), being "preparatory to another state of things." Ellicott sees in the verse "the essence of Christianity as distinct from Judaism" — "a spiritual religion developed from within" against an external code.

The Reformed Calvin reads it as "an anticipation" meeting the Jews' over-esteem of Moses, showing "how far inferior the ministry of Moses was to the power of Christ" — yet "this comparison sheds no small lustre on the power of Christ," since what Moses brought was "exceedingly small, when compared with the grace of Christ." Gill spells out the law's office, moral and ceremonial: it "discovers sin, accuses of it, convicts of it, and condemns for it; nor could it give strength to perform its demands; nor does it give the least hint of forgiveness."

The Wesleyan Wesley admires the exactness of the wording — "no philosopher, poet, or orator, ever chose his words so accurately" — observing that "the law of Moses was not his own; the grace of Christ was," with "his grace opposite to the wrath, his truth to the shadowy ceremonies of the law." Clarke names Christ "the mediator of the new covenant, as Moses was of the old" (Hebrews 8:6; Galatians 3:19). The schools confess one movement of revelation: shadow giving way to substance in Christ.
18:
On v.18 the witnesses are united that God is invisible and that Christ alone reveals him, and they touch the famous textual variant and the meaning of "seen."

The grammatical-historical commentators establish the invisibility: "God no one has ever seen" with the physical eye (Robertson), who gathers Exodus 33:20, Deuteronomy 4:12, and Colossians 1:15, and notes the disputed reading "only begotten God" against the Received "only begotten Son." Barnes explains that Christ "had a knowledge of God which might be expressed... by saying that he saw him intimately and completely," and was "therefore fitted" to reveal him, surpassing Moses and all the prophets. The Wesleyan reading qualifies the verb: Clarke takes "seen" as "known" (as in 1 John 3:6), so that "no man, how highly soever favoured, hath fully known God" apart from Christ, while Wesley adds that, though God is not seen "with bodily eyes," yet "believers see him with the eye of faith."

The Greek fathers dwell on the intimacy of "in the bosom of the Father." Chrysostom insists this is "much more than simply to see": "he who sees simply, has not the knowledge thoroughly of that which he sees; but he who dwells in the bosom, knows every thing" — so the Son's knowledge of the Father is not partial but complete. Cyril of Jerusalem guards the doctrine with the Old Testament theophanies — even Ezekiel "saw the likeness of the glory of the Lord, not the Lord Himself," for "the incorporeal cannot be subject to bodily sight." Origen notes the dispute (against Heracleon) over whether these are the Baptist's words or the evangelist's, but lets the confession stand.

The Reformed Calvin draws the practical conclusion: "the knowledge of God is the door by which we enter into the enjoyment of all blessings; and as it is by Christ alone that God makes himself known to us, hence too it follows that we ought to seek all things from Christ." Gill adds that "no man hath seen God" speaks of the Father, while "the Only Begotten" frequently "appeared to the Old Testament saints, in an human form." The verse stands, across the schools, as the prologue's seal: the unseen God is "declared" (exēgēsato) by the only-begotten who dwells in his bosom.
19:
v.19 opens the formal testimony of John, and the witnesses set the scene: "the Jews" of Jerusalem send priests and Levites to ask, "Who art thou?"

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the Johannine term. Ellicott notes that "the Jews," originally the men of the tribe of Judah, was "extended after the Captivity to the whole nation," and is used by this evangelist "more than seventy times," generally of "the representatives of the nation" in their opposition to Christ. Robertson calls this testimony "the most important item to add after the Prologue," observing that the Gospel here assumes the Synoptic account of the baptism and fills out "our knowledge of this first year" of the ministry. The Puritan Henry identifies the senders as "the great sanhedrim or high-commission court... which was the representative of the Jewish church, who took cognizance of all matters relating to religion," and marks the irony that they "who were the fountains of learning, and the guides" of the people must send to inquire.

The Alexandrian Origen sees a fitness in the deputation: "the Jews of Jerusalem, as being of kin to the Baptist, who was of the priestly stock, send Priests and Levites," and he counts this the second of John's testimonies. There is a quiet debate over motive. "It is generally believed," Calvin reports, "that, out of hatred to Christ, they gave to John an honour which did not belong to him"; but he disputes this, holding the inquiry to be the natural business of those charged to examine a new teacher and a new rite. The Latin Augustine lifts the scene to its theme: John, "the friend of the Bridegroom," was "zealous for the Bridegroom, not for himself; not seeking his own honour, but that of his Judge, whom as a herald he preceded" — granted the unique grace not merely to predict Christ, as the prophets did, but "to point Him out." Gill simply notes that the evangelist now gives "a large, and full account" of the testimony he had earlier announced. The schools agree that the official summons becomes the occasion for the herald to efface himself before the Coming One.
20:
On v.20 the witnesses dwell on the emphatic threefold construction — "he confessed, and denied not, but confessed" — as the mark of John's frank and unhesitating self-denial. The grammatical-historical commentators note the Johannine habit of pairing a positive statement with its negative (Robertson, who marks the verbs homologeō, to confess, and arneomai, to deny), and the emphatic pronoun: the better reading places it first, so that the sense is "It is not I who am the Messiah" (Ellicott) — he understands their unspoken question, "Is it thou who art the Christ?"

Barnes draws from this a strong proof of John's honesty. His "fame was great," and "the nation was expecting that the Messiah was about to come, and multitudes were ready to believe that John was he" (Luke 3:15); had he been "an impostor he would have taken advantage of this excited state of public feeling, proclaimed himself to be the Messiah, and formed a large party in his favour. The fact that he did not do it is full proof that he did not intend to impose on men." The Reformed Gill and Calvin stress the openness of the confession — made "freely, and without any reserve," "in the plainest and strongest terms," and "without any ambiguity or hypocrisy"; Calvin notes that the word "confessed" is first used generally, that John "stated the fact as it really was," and then repeated "to express the form of the confession," in which "he replied expressly."

The Wesleyan Clarke gives it the pastoral point: "John renounces himself, that Jesus may be all in all"; though "God had highly honoured him," he "considered he had nothing but what he had received, and therefore, giving all praise to his benefactor," he took care "to direct the attention of the people to him alone." Wesley adds the historical note that the denial was needed because "many supposed he was" the Christ. The comment is brief, but the schools speak with one voice: the forerunner's greatness shows precisely in the firmness with which he says what he is not. Gill adds that John "did not retract his confession," nor "drop any thing that looked doubtful or suspicious," and Clarke notes that the doubled "confessed... denied not... confessed" is "a common mode of Jewish phraseology."
21:
v.21 raises a celebrated crux: John denies being Elijah, yet Jesus says "this is Elijah which is to come" (Matthew 11:14; cf. Matthew 17:10-12; Malachi 4:5). The witnesses resolve it with one accord by distinguishing the personal from the spiritual sense.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain that the Jews "expected that Elijah would appear before the Messiah came" — "the real Elijah returned from heaven" in person; "in this sense John denied that he was Elijah," while still being the Elijah of prophecy who came "in the spirit and power of Elias" (Luke 1:17) to "prepare the way" (Barnes, who notes that Jesus accordingly calls him the Elias of Matthew 3:3). Robertson adds that in Mark 9:11-13 Jesus identifies John with Malachi's Elijah, "the forerunner of the Messiah," so that John denies only the expectation that "Elijah would return in person." The "other prophet" they ask after is the prophet like Moses of Deuteronomy 18:15, whom they held distinct from the Messiah — though, says Clarke, "the prophet... promised by Moses was no other than the Messiah himself" — and Lightfoot notes the rabbinic expectation that "the prophets were to rise again" at the Messiah's coming, the spirit of prophecy having "departed from Israel after... Zechariah and Malachi."

The Reformed Calvin traces the confusion to a false opinion: the Jews, "holding the opinion that the soul of a man departs out of one body into another," imagined "that the same Elias who lived under the reign of king Ahab" (1 Kings 17:1) "was to come"; John rightly denies that, while owning the office. The Alexandrian Origen frames the difficulty directly, recalling Jesus' "if ye will receive it, this is Elijah which is to come." The Wesleyan Wesley puts it tersely: "He was not that Elijah (the Tishbite) of whom they spoke." The schools meet in one resolution: John is not Elijah in person, but is Elijah in office and in spirit — the appointed forerunner. Clarke records the further rabbinic expectation that "Jeremiah was to return to life, and restore the pot of manna," which coloured their question about "that prophet."
22:
v.22 is the hinge of the interrogation: having heard only who he is not, the deputation presses for a positive answer — "Who art thou? that we may give an answer to them that sent us. What sayest thou of thyself?" The witnesses treat the verse briefly and with one mind.

The Alexandrian Origen paraphrases the emissaries' logic: "We had a surmise what you were and came to learn if it was so; but now we know that you are not that. It remains for us, therefore, to hear your account of yourself, so that we may report your answer to those who sent us." The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note the form of the verse: Robertson marks the second aorist "they said" (eipan) and the inferential "therefore" (oun), the question now briefer than in v.19, with the final clause "that we may give answer" (hina apokrisin dōmen) and the dative "to those that sent." Ellicott observes that John "has given the No to all the ideas they had formed of him," so that "there is nothing left to them but to draw the definite statement from himself, or to return to their senders empty handed."

The Reformed Gill explains that, "since as yet he had only answered... who he was not — that he was not the Christ, nor Elias, nor that prophet — they desire he would give them a positive account who he was." The Wesleyan Clarke adds the historical setting: these were "probably a deputation from the grand Sanhedrin," who, "hearing of the success of the Baptist's preaching," were "puzzled to know what to make of him, and seriously desired to hear from himself what he professed to be." It is a transitional verse, and the schools simply mark the official accountability of the messengers — answerable to those who sent them — which forces from John the self-effacing confession of v.23. Robertson notes that the noun "answer" (apokrisis) recurs in Luke 2:47, and that the deputation, bound to report, can rest in nothing less than John's own word about himself.
23:
On v.23 the witnesses agree that John answers by quoting Isaiah 40:3 of himself — "I am the voice of one crying in the wilderness" — and that the self-description is a marvel of humility.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that the Synoptics (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3) apply Isaiah's words to John, but here John "both expresses them, and interprets them of himself"; Robertson sees "no reason to think that he did not do so." Ellicott draws out the self-effacement: John "still gives the No. They think of his person and his work. He thinks of neither. His eye is fixed on the coming One. In this presence his own personality has no existence. He is as a voice, not to be inquired about but heard" — and he compares men who waste time "asking questions about the messenger of a great king who is coming," instead of hastening "to make ready for him." The Wesleyan Wesley makes the image vivid: "Far from being Christ, or even Elijah, I am nothing but a voice: a sound that so soon as it has expressed the thought of which it is the sign, dies into air, and is known no more."

The Reformed Gill and Calvin ground the answer in his commission. Calvin notes that John "would have been chargeable with rashness in undertaking the office of teaching, if he had not received a commission," so he "proves it by a quotation from the Prophet Isaiah," who, "promising the restoration of the Church," foretold "joyful voices, commanding to prepare the way." Gill adds that John spoke "under the infallible direction of the blessed Spirit," which "confirms the sense of the evangelists" in applying the words to him. Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the same Isaiah with its glad sequel — "your redemption hath drawn nigh" — and the call, "Ho, ye that thirst, come ye to the water." The schools confess one voice in the wilderness, content to be heard and then to vanish before the Word. Cyril of Jerusalem continues the prophet's call — "Hearken unto me, and ye shall eat that which is good" — joining the wilderness voice to the feast of the gospel.
24:
v.24 notes that "they which were sent were of the Pharisees," and the witnesses read it as the evangelist's way of explaining the sharper question that follows in v.25.

The grammatical-historical commentators make a telling observation about this Gospel's usage. Ellicott notes that the best manuscripts read "they had been sent from the Pharisees," and that, since the Sadducees are "nowhere mentioned" in John, "the term Pharisees seems to be used almost in the sense of Sanhedrin" (cf. John 4:1; John 11:57). Robertson marks the periphrastic past perfect "had been sent" and the source-preposition "from" (ek) the Pharisees. Barnes finds in this incidental detail "one of those... delicate allusions which would occur to no impostor in forging a book": the Pharisees, not the Sadducees, send the deputation — and elsewhere (Matthew 3:7) the Sadducees come to John — a mark "that the writers of the New Testament were honest men and knew what they affirmed."

The Alexandrian Origen distinguishes this from the earlier deputation: noting "the mildness of the former question, so befitting the priestly and levitical character," he sees the Pharisees coming with a harder, more censorious temper. The Reformed Calvin makes the same point doctrinally — these were "men clothed with authority," not "some contemptible persons of the order of the Levites," and therefore "they raise a question about his office" and "accuse him of rashness for venturing to introduce a new religious observance." Gill describes the Pharisees as "the straitest sect," "zealous of the traditions of the elders," professing "expectation of the Messiah," and "famous... for their knowledge and learning." The Wesleyan Wesley adds that they were "peculiarly tenacious of old customs, and jealous of any innovation," except such as "their own scribes" brought in. The verse is scene-setting, but the schools agree it prepares the challenge to John's very authority to baptize. Barnes observes that, by contrast, "many of the Sadducees came to his baptism" (Matthew 3:7), yet "did not join in sending to him" — the Pharisees alone send this deputation, a quiet token of the writers' accuracy.
25:
On v.25 the witnesses agree that the Pharisees' question turns on authority: "Why baptizest thou then, if thou be not that Christ, nor Elias, neither that prophet?"

The Reformed Calvin lays out their reasoning as a syllogism of "three degrees": "it does not belong to every man to institute the practice of baptism"; it would befit only "the Messiah," who "was to be one who possessed all authority" and would "commence the restoration both of the royal authority and of the Church," or Elijah, or "the prophets of God," who "have a right to discharge the office committed to them"; and since John claims to be none of these, "they conclude that for John to baptize is an unlawful novelty." The grammatical-historical commentators locate the true scandal in the nature of the rite. Robertson, citing Abrahams, notes that "proselyte baptism was probably practised before John's time," so that "its use by John was treating the Jews as if they were themselves Gentiles." Ellicott agrees: baptism was "one of the initiatory rites of proselytes," and the offence is that John "places Jews and even Pharisees in an analogous position to that of proselytes, and makes them to pass through a rite which marks them out as impure, and needing to be cleansed."

Lightfoot grounds this in Jewish expectation: they "expected the world should be renewed at the coming of the Messiah" and looked for "the purifying of the unclean," citing the promise of cleansing water in Ezekiel 36:25. Clarke adds that the Jews "never received a proselyte... till he was both baptized and circumcised," and that "such baptisms were never performed except by an ordinance of the Sanhedrin, or in the presence of three magistrates." The Wesleyan Wesley presses the procedural objection — John baptized "without any commission from the Sanhedrim," and baptized not "only heathens... but Jews also." The Alexandrian Origen weighs whether this forms a distinct, third testimony. The schools agree: the challenge is not to baptism as such, but to John's right, lacking the recognised offices, to summon Israel itself to be washed. Lightfoot grounds the question in the rabbinic hope of a Messianic cleansing, citing Ezekiel 36:26 — "I will... remove your uncleanness, by the sprinkling of the water of purification."
26:
v.26 gives John's answer — "I baptize with water: but there standeth one among you, whom ye know not" — and the witnesses hear in it both the modesty of his rite and the tragedy of Israel's ignorance.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress that John does not defend his authority but deflects it: his is merely water-baptism, a preparatory sign, while the greater One is already present and unrecognised. Robertson notes the adjective "in the midst of you standeth" (mesos hymōn) and observes that John "had already baptized Jesus and recognized him as the Messiah," so that "whom ye know not" is "the tragedy of the situation," already sounded in v.11. Barnes draws the implication: John "did not deny it, nor did he condescend to state his authority," but "admitted that he had introduced an important change," and warns that greater changes "would soon take place without their authority," for "the Messiah was about to come, and the power was about to depart from their hands." Ellicott hears behind the words the contrast of Ezekiel 36:25 between water and the Spirit.

The Reformed Gill and Calvin see the answer pointing wholly away from John to Christ. Gill says John owns his baptism to be "a new rite," of which he is "the administrator," yet "suggests... that there was one at hand, and even now among them, that should baptize." Calvin draws the larger inference: "when he sends them to Christ, and declares that Christ is present, this is a clear proof... that he is the true voice, who is sent to testify that the time is come for the renovation of the Church"; the unstated contrast is that the One who follows baptizes with the Spirit. The Wesleyan Clarke adds the geographical note that the One "whose forerunner I am is now dwelling in the land of Judea, and will shortly make his appearance" — though "not present" at this moment (cf. John 1:29). The Alexandrian Origen defends the relevance of the reply against Heracleon, who thought John answered "not to what they asked, but to what he wished." The schools confess one point: John's water only prepares for the unknown Christ who already stands among them.
27:
The witnesses divide their work cleanly: the grammatical-historical commentators supply a text-critical note, and the Reformed voices draw the doctrine. With one mind the philologists judge the clause "who is preferred before me" to be a scribal insertion borrowed from v.15 and v.30; omitting it, the saying runs simply, "He who cometh after me, the thong of whose sandal I am not worthy to unloose, standeth among you, and ye know him not" (Ellicott, with Clarke citing Griesbach). Ellicott draws out the force of the corrected text: it is "the authority for baptism, the outer sign of the Messiah's advent," for the coming One already stands in their midst — John's work is simply ministerial, and the baptism of the Spirit is at hand.

They press the image, too. To loose the sandal-thong was the lowest service of a slave, and Barnes draws the pastoral point that John, honoured and thronged with hearers while Jesus was still unknown, counts himself unworthy even of that office — "so we all should be willing to lay all that we have at the feet of Christ." Robertson adds the philological parallels in the Baptist's saying elsewhere (Mark 1:7, Matthew 3:11, Acts 13:25), noting this is the only place John joins axios ("worthy") with a hina-clause.

The Reformed expositors (Calvin, Gill) read the same words doctrinally. Gill observes that John was before Christ both in the world and in ministry, yet "greatly behind him" in all else. Calvin finds two things asserted and a third soon to follow: Christ comes after John in order of time, yet stands far before him in rank and dignity, and at last is preferred to all "because he is in reality more exalted than all others" — John being nothing but a herald sent on Christ's account.

One Eastern outlier turns the verse elsewhere: Cyril of Jerusalem hears in the Baptist's "I am not worthy" an echo of Paul's confession that he was "not worthy to be called an apostle" because he had persecuted the church (1 Corinthians 15:9) — a homiletical aside on humility rather than a comment on the text itself.
28:
The whole cloud of witnesses fastens on a single geographical and text-critical question: did John write "in Bethany beyond Jordan" or "in Bethabara"? The grammatical-historical majority is now firmly for Bethany. Robertson calls it "undoubtedly the correct text," read by all known Greek manuscripts; Ellicott and Barnes agree that Bethabara is a later substitution. They trace the change to Origen, who — unable in his day to locate a Bethany on the Jordan — preferred Bethabara, a ford he had heard of, on the strength of its etymology and a local baptism tradition. Barnes notes that the genuine Bethany "beyond Jordan" lay in the tribe of Reuben, distinct from the Bethany near Jerusalem.

The Alexandrian Origen himself supplies the dissenting case in his own words: he concedes that "almost all the copies" read Bethany, but says he visited the sites, found the Bethany near Jerusalem far too distant from the river, and judged the true reading to be Bethabara, whose name yields "House of preparation," fitting for him who prepared a people for the Lord, while Bethany means "House of obedience." The Antiochene Chrysostom stands with him, holding that "the more correct copies have Bethabara," since the Jerusalem Bethany was not beyond Jordan nor near the wilderness. Clarke, weighing the fathers, also leans to Bethabara as "the house of passage," thought to be where Israel crossed under Joshua.

The Reformed Calvin declines to adjudicate the spelling, noting only that the place is named to authenticate the narrative and to show that John's testimony was given before a great concourse at his usual place of baptizing — some, he adds, connecting it with the memorable crossing of Jordan under Joshua. Gill and Wesley keep it briefest of all, Wesley noting only that John "used to baptize" there. Ellicott presses the point of method against Origen: the evangelist writes as an eye-witness who fixes days and hours, and a place near Galilee suits the sequence into v.43 and John 2:1; ignorance of a site after three centuries of war is no warrant for overturning the manuscripts. Chrysostom adds the pastoral note that the very naming of the spot — in the midst of the multitude, not in a corner — displays the boldness of the loud-voiced herald.
29:
Here the witnesses are, at root, unanimous: across the Greek and Latin fathers, the Reformers, the Wesleyans, and the modern grammarians, the Baptist's cry — "Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world" — proclaims Christ the true sacrifice in whom atonement is made and man reconciled to God. The schools differ in approach, not in doctrine.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the sacrificial background. With one voice they hear behind "the Lamb" the daily morning-and-evening offering (Exodus 29:38), the Passover lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7), and the silent victim of Isaiah 53:7. Lightfoot anchors it in the rite of laying hands on the victim's head (Leviticus 1:4), transferring guilt, so that Christ "himself bare our sins in his own body" (1 Peter 2:24). Robertson notes the present tense ho airon — a continual bearing-away — and the singular "sin," the whole mass of it; Ellicott observes that the verb holds both meanings, that Christ takes sin away precisely by bearing it (1 John 3:5).

The Reformed voices read doctrinally: Calvin makes this the chief office of Christ, who by the sacrifice of his death appeases the Father's wrath and is reckoned the one true propitiation, overturning every human satisfaction and purification; "of the world" extends the benefit indiscriminately to the whole human race, yet it is received only by the guidance of faith. Gill and the Wesleyan voices (Wesley, Clarke) press the same universal scope — "sin and the world are of equal extent" — against any narrowing to Israel (1 John 2:2). The Latin Augustine strikes the note that only Christ is the spotless Lamb, "not conceived in iniquity" (Psalm 51:5); no sinful minister, however holy, takes away another's sin — Christ alone does. The Puritan Matthew Henry turns it pastoral: "if Christ takes away the sin of the world, then why not my sin?"

The Alexandrian Origen is the outlier, layering allegory over the plain type: the lamb as the daily "reasonable" sacrifice, the morning and evening offerings figuring the soul's ascent to and descent from divine things. But this is a deepening, not a dissent — every school confesses the one Lamb who bears away the sin of the world.
30:
The comment here is thin, the witnesses treating the verse as the Baptist's deliberate repetition of a saying already heard. The grammatical-historical voices note that "This is he of whom I said" meets us now for the third time, after v.15 and v.27. Ellicott reads it as a quotation of an earlier, unrecorded and proverbial statement of John, "to be understood in their fulfilment," comparing the Baptist's later "he must increase, but I must decrease" (John 3:30). Robertson observes the emphatic preposition hyper hou, "on behalf of whom," as the Baptist points to Jesus standing before them — "This is he. There he is" — and refers back to v.15 for the fuller discussion. Gill places the original saying earlier still, before ever Christ came to be baptized and before John personally knew him.

The Reformed Calvin gathers the whole confession into three claims, comprehending everything "in a few words." First, that John went before Christ only in order of time, as the messenger sent before his face to prepare the way (Malachi 3:1); from this it follows that John is nothing more than a herald sent on Christ's account, and that Christ is the Messiah. Second, that Christ is "preferred" before him — this relating to the glory with which God adorned his Son when he came as Redeemer. Third, the reason underlying both: that Christ is far superior in dignity, a precedence which, Calvin insists, "was not accidental, but was due to his eternal majesty." So the verse, slight in itself, carries for the Reformed the weight of Christ's pre-existent divine being. Robertson sends the reader back to v.15 for the fuller treatment of these very words, and Gill locates the original saying as early as John's first baptizing, before ever Christ came to him; so that all four witnesses, with no debate among them, hear the verse as the forerunner subordinating himself a third time — behind in time, beneath in rank, and pointing wholly beyond himself to the One whose precedence rests on eternity. Ellicott compares the Baptist's later confession that "he must increase, but I must decrease" (John 3:30), the same self-effacing note sounded once more.
31:
The witnesses agree closely. "I knew him not," they hold, does not deny all acquaintance but disclaims the kind of personal intimacy that could make John's testimony a put-up job. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the circumstance: John had lived in the hill country and the desert, Jesus in obscurity at Nazareth, so that until Jesus came to be baptized there was no personal acquaintance between them — which, Barnes argues, "proves that there could have been no collusion or agreement between them to impose on the people." Clarke notes John had spent thirty years in the hill country of Hebron while Jesus remained in obscure Nazareth. Ellicott nuances the phrase: John did not know him standing among them as Messiah, though as kinsman he must have known the son of Mary, his features and the wondrous incidents of his birth — reconciling the words with John's reluctance at the baptism (Matthew 3:13). Robertson takes the pluperfect "I knew him not" as an imperfect and stresses the stated purpose: the whole point of John's water-baptism was "that he should be made manifest to Israel."

The Wesleyan voices (Wesley, Clarke) marvel at the providence that kept the two kinsmen apart — John "in the deserts till the day of his showing unto Israel" (Luke 1:80), some ninety miles from Nazareth — precisely so that his witness could not be suspected of family partiality. The Reformed Calvin presses the same defence further into doctrine: John speaks not by his own suggestion or for any human favour but by the Spirit and the command of God who sent him; and from John's being sent Calvin infers that no man may institute sacraments of his own accord — the right belongs to God alone, as Christ argued from the very baptism of John (Matthew 21:25). Thus a verse of personal disclaimer becomes, for the Reformed, a charter of the lawful ministry. Gill adds that the providential separation forestalls any suspicion that John's high commendation of Jesus "arose from the relation between them, or from a confederacy and compact"; and Robertson notes that the Synoptic account of the baptism is presupposed throughout the passage.
32:
On the Spirit descending "as a dove" and abiding on Jesus, the witnesses agree on the fact and divide on its manner and meaning, the Latin fathers most active. Augustine, cited at length in the Catena, insists the dove was a real body, not a deceiving appearance, for the Spirit could no more deceive than the Son; and that this was not Christ's first reception of the Spirit — whom he had from his very conception — but a manifestation. The dove signifies simplicity, unity, and peace ("my dove is one"), as the cloven tongues of fire later signify fervour: the one shape warning against guile, the other against coldness. He reads the whole scene as the open showing of the Trinity — the Father in the voice, the Son baptized, the Spirit in the dove (Matthew 3:16) — and even hears in the dove's moaning the Spirit who "maketh intercession for us with groanings that cannot be uttered." Gregory adds the careful distinction that the Spirit "abode" on the Mediator alone perpetually, whereas in the elect his sanctifying gifts abide but his charismatic gifts may withdraw, that men may be kept humble.

The grammatical-historical voices keep to the sober ground: the dove was the appointed Semitic symbol of the Spirit (Robertson), and John's perfect tense, "I have beheld," registers the sign as abiding, conclusive proof; the account presupposes the Synoptic baptism (Mark 1:10). The Reformed Calvin reads the seeing figuratively, by the same logic as the sacraments: as the bread is called Christ's body, so the visible dove is "the Spirit," a sure and efficacious sign though the Spirit fills heaven and earth (Jeremiah 23:24). The dove's form, he urges, displays the mildness foretold of the Messiah who will not break the bruised reed (Isaiah 42:3); and Christ received the Spirit not for himself but to pour out on his people. The Antiochene Chrysostom secures the same guard: the descent took place not because Christ lacked the Spirit but to manifest him and fix the Father's voice upon his head; and if the Jews did not believe though they saw, it is because such sights require the mind's eye, as the visions of Daniel, Ezekiel, and Moses were not seen by all.
33:
The verse turns on the appointed sign — "upon whom thou shalt see the Spirit descending and remaining, the same baptizeth with the Holy Ghost" — and the witnesses agree it was God's pre-given token by which John would infallibly know the Messiah. The grammatical-historical voices set the contrast plainly: John baptizes "in water," the coming One "in the Holy Spirit" (Robertson, citing Mark 1:8); the descent was the divinely fixed mark, not John's own conjecture (Barnes), the same emphatic "He" pointing back to the God who sent John in v.6. Clarke draws from it that John enjoyed continual immediate inspiration from God — receiving "not only his call and mission at first, but every subsequent direction" — and asks who is fit to proclaim Jesus but one who has such continual intercourse with God.

The Reformed Calvin meets the difficulty head-on: if John did not know Christ, why did he refuse to baptize him, saying "I have need to be baptized of thee" (Matthew 3:14)? He answers that the "knowledge" denied is the intimacy of long, personal acquaintance, and that the dove-sign was added not for John's sake alone but for ours — a confirmation given after the event, after the manner of the token granted to Moses (Exodus 3:12), which assured the deliverance when it came to pass.

The Latin Augustine grounds John's reliability in the doctrine of the Trinity: "he that speaketh a lie speaketh of his own," but John is true because sent by the Truth, that is, by Christ — "and what the Father does with Christ, Christ does," inseparable in love, unity, and majesty. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem adds the catechetical note that "the heavens were opened" at the dignity of the One descending, and that the dove's form befits the pure and innocent Spirit who helps the prayers of those born again. Barnes allows that before the baptism John already supposed and believed Jesus to be the Messiah (Matthew 3:13), yet held that "the infallible, certain testimony" was reserved for the descent of the Spirit; and Gill speaks of the sign being "secretly suggested to him who he was."
34:
The verse is the conclusion of the Baptist's witness — "I saw, and bare record, that this is the Son of God" — and the witnesses read it as the goal toward which all the signs pointed. The grammatical-historical voices mark the perfect tenses, "I have seen" and "I have borne witness" (Robertson, Ellicott): the vision issues in an abiding testimony, teacher and learner now agreeing — as Ellicott puts it, "teacher has now learnt, and learner is now taught" — that Jesus is this pre-existent Being, the Messiah, the Son of God. Robertson weighs the title with care: "Son of God" serves as a Messianic designation on the lips of Nathanael (v.49), Martha (John 11:27), and even Caiaphas under oath (Matthew 26:63), yet here and throughout this Gospel it expresses "more than just Messiah," the peculiar relation of the Son to the Father (John 5:25; John 20:31). Gill notes simply that John saw the Spirit descend and remain "with his bodily eyes."

The Reformed Calvin draws the inference tight: God made John "fully and thoroughly acquainted" with what he was to witness, and because the one who confers the Holy Spirit must be the Christ — to whom alone belongs the office of reconciling men to God — John's seeing of the Spirit certifies the divine Sonship. Wesley notes that John now confesses more than he had received from report, both the person and the office of Christ. The Latin Augustine sounds, more obliquely, a contemplative register, recalling that "all flesh is grass... but the word of the Lord abideth for ever," the eternal Word being the only object worth loving unto eternity — a pastoral meditation alongside the others' direct exegesis. Robertson ranges the title's other occurrences — Caiaphas' adjuration and Jesus' sworn reply (Matthew 26:64), and the mutual knowing of Father and Son (Matthew 11:27) — while Calvin insists that "to no other belongs the honour and the office of reconciling men to God," so that the gift of the Spirit and the divine Sonship stand or fall together.
35:
On the next day, with John standing and two of his disciples, the witnesses dwell on the Baptist's self-effacing handing-over of his followers to Christ. The Latin and Catena fathers read it richly. Augustine makes John "the friend of the bridegroom," who sought not his own glory but bore witness to the truth, and so would not keep his disciples to the hindrance of their following the Lord, but showed them whom to follow. Bede pictures John "standing" upon the citadel of all the virtues, "from which no temptations could cast him down," his disciples standing with him as stout-hearted followers; and reads the walking of Jesus as "the economy of the Incarnation," by which he condescended to come to us. Alcuin adds a mystical tableau — the Law (John) now stands still, the Law having ceased, while Jesus "walks" to gather disciples and bring the grace of the Gospel. The Antiochene voices add that Theophylact heard in John's gaze his "love and admiration for Christ," while Chrysostom presses why John waited by the river rather than going about to preach: had he gone about, "it would have seemed like human partiality," and suspicion would have been excited; instead he willed that Christ's own works should draw men, and "struck a small spark" which suddenly rose into a flame.

The grammatical-historical voices keep the historical frame. The "again" looks back to v.29 (Ellicott, Robertson); the past-perfect "was standing" sets the scene; and the two disciples are Andrew, named in v.40, and the unnamed Beloved Disciple, John himself, "who records this incident with happy memories." Barnes notes the testimony is repeated because the evangelist's main design is to show that Jesus is the Messiah, and Ellicott adds the pastoral observation that within John's wider band there was an inner circle, taught more because able to receive more. Chrysostom observes that the prophets and apostles preached Christ absent, but here he was to be "pointed out by the eye, not by the voice only"; and Clarke remarks that the unnamed second disciple is John himself, who "in every thing in which he might receive honour... studiously endeavours to conceal his own name."
36:
John, looking on Jesus as he walked, repeats "Behold the Lamb of God." The grammatical-historical voices fasten on the fixed, earnest gaze: the participle emblepsas (Robertson, Clarke, Ellicott) pictures John's rapt contemplation, and Robertson notes this is, so far as we know, the third and last glimpse of Jesus by the Baptist, who simply repeats part of the tribute of v.29. Clarke draws the devotional lesson that only such steadfast looking discovers the glory of the Lamb: at first sight he appears "only as a man among men," but on attentive consideration "no less than God manifest in the flesh," and by his death making atonement for the sin of the world — the Baptist pointing his hearers to Christ both as the great sacrifice and as "the complete teacher of heavenly truth." Barnes turns it to the ministry: so should all preachers fix the eye on the Son of God and direct all others to him.

The Reformed Calvin reads the repetition as John's deliberate self-resignation: sensing his course nearly run, he labours over many successive days to hand his office to Christ, and his very firmness and constancy lend greater credit to his witness. Calvin marks how small and lowly the church's beginning was — John prepares disciples, but Christ now begins to collect a Church with "no more than two men who are mean and unknown" — which only illustrates his glory, that within a short period, without human aid and without a strong hand, he would spread his kingdom "in a wonderful and incredible manner." And he fixes on the chief object to which John directs men: to find in Christ the forgiveness of sins. This gracious invitation, once made to two persons, Calvin says, "now belongs to all"; we need not fear that Christ will refuse us easy access, for he "seeks at a distance those who are wandering and astray, that he may bring them back to the right road." Gill pictures the Baptist fixing his eyes "with great pleasure and delight," pointing at Christ as the people departed at the close of day; the gesture is for Barnes the very model of how "all ministers of the gospel" should fix the eye on the Son of God and direct others to him.
37:
The two disciples hear John and follow Jesus, and the witnesses agree this is the first ingathering of Christ's church. The Antiochene Chrysostom probes why they moved now and not before: it was the humbler word, "Behold the Lamb," touching on what pertains to men's salvation, that drew them where talk of majesty had not — "for many persons are less influenced by the thoughts of God's greatness than when they hear of His being man's Helper and Friend." He draws his great image of John as the bridegroom's friend who silently puts the bride's hand into Christ's and is forgotten thereafter, and notes the disciples' modesty and zeal in seeking private converse rather than interrogating him in public. The Latin and Catena fathers (Augustine, Alcuin) read "Come and see" mystically — come by believing and working, and then see by understanding — while the Alexandrian Origen, characteristically, counts the testimonies, finding that after six witnesses John falls silent and Jesus asks "seventhly," "What seek ye?"

The grammatical-historical voices keep the narrative ground: the ingressive aorist "they followed" marks the decisive step (Robertson); Barnes makes John's willingness to lose his disciples to Christ the proof of his sincerity — "he was not desirous of forming a party or of building up a sect" — and the lesson that ministers should point men past themselves to the Saviour. Wesley notes they followed but "had not the courage to speak to him."

The Puritan Matthew Henry expands the scene pastorally and at length. These two are "the first-fruits of Christ's disciples"; Christ "takes early cognizance of the first motions of a soul towards him" (Luke 15:20); his question "What seek ye?" is no reprimand but a kind invitation, the very question every beginner should put to himself — for "those that follow Christ, and yet seek the world, or themselves... deceive themselves." And the moment to act is now: "strike while the iron is hot," for "now is the accepted time" (2 Corinthians 6:2).
