Ellicott marks the tact of the moment: there is "no word cutting the link" between John and his disciples, and none bidding them follow Jesus, "for that would have been hard to speak, hard to hear" — the passing voice simply yielding to the abiding Word.
38:
Jesus turns, sees them following, and asks "What seek ye?"; they answer, "Rabbi, where dwellest thou?" The grammatical-historical voices dwell on the details. Robertson marks the vivid strapheis, picturing Jesus suddenly turning at the sound of their steps, and notes that "What seek ye?" are "the first words of Jesus preserved in this Gospel." He observes the disciples first address Jesus as Rabbi while others say Kyrie, and that Mary will later say Rabbouni (John 20:16). Both he and Ellicott stress that the evangelist translates "Rabbi" for his Greek and later readers (methermeneuomenon), Ellicott adding that it was a modern word, known to the Jews only since the days of Hillel and not to Greeks at all. The disciples, they agree, do not seek a passing word but a place for quiet converse. Lightfoot, more speculatively, renders "where dost thou keep sabbath?" and conjectures the day was a sabbath-eve.

Barnes draws the verse out pastorally: the question was "not asked to obtain information" but as "a kind inquiry," an invitation to lay open their minds; Jesus "regards the first inclination of the soul to follow him," and ministers in turn should be "accessible, kind, and tender" to all who inquire the way to life. Religion, he adds, "teaches men true politeness," a disposition not to molest others when engaged.

The Reformed Calvin reads "Rabbi" as the title then given to teachers and expounders of the word: moved by John's commendation, the two already hold Jesus a prophet and teacher, "which is the first step towards receiving instruction." From their seeking his dwelling rather than a mere passing look, Calvin draws the lesson that we must not be content to "smell the gospel at a distance," but seek to lodge with Christ — for during that night, he is sure, Jesus instructed them more fully and bound them to himself. Clarke turns the question inward, asking what we seek "in the company you frequent... in the works which you perform" — the illumination, justification, and sanctification of the soul, or merely ourselves; and Robertson concludes simply that "they wished a place for quiet converse with Jesus."
39:
"Come and see... and they abode with him that day: it was about the tenth hour." The witnesses agree on the gracious immediacy of Christ's welcome — he does not, Barnes notes, "put them off to some future period," but admits at once those who seek him — while dividing sharply over the hour. The question is whether John reckons by Jewish time (counting from sunrise, so the tenth hour is about 4 p.m.) or by Roman time (about 10 a.m.). Clarke, following Macknight, argues at length for the Roman ten in the morning — citing Josephus and Horace on the reckoning of hours — since the disciples "abode with him that day" implies a considerable time, useless to note if only two hours of a Jewish day remained. Robertson agrees, holding that John, writing late at Ephesus, naturally uses Roman reckoning, as at John 20:19. On the other side, Ellicott keeps the Babylonian and Jewish method — about four in the afternoon — and the Reformed Calvin agrees: "the evening was approaching, for it was not more than two hours till sunset," the disciples so eager that they "gave themselves no concern about a night's lodging." Barnes lays out both computations and leaves the matter open.

Across the divide the grammatical-historical voices share a devotional reading. Robertson observes that "to his latest day John never forgot the hour when first he met Jesus," and Clarke turns "Come and see" into an invitation to find where Christ truly dwells — not "in the tumult of worldly affairs," nor in profane assemblies or idle pleasure, but "in the humble, contrite spirit, in the spirit of faith, of love, of forgiveness." Calvin presses the rebuke home: the disciples' eagerness shames our reluctance, "for we incessantly delay, because it is not convenient for us to follow Christ." Barnes states the alternatives without deciding — four in the afternoon by Jewish reckoning, ten in the forenoon by Roman, "it is not certain which he used" — while Ellicott finds "no sufficient reason" to think the Babylonian counting usual at Ephesus is here departed from, fixing the writer's own remembered turning-point at four o'clock.
40:
The verse names "one of the two... Andrew, Simon Peter's brother," and the comment, though brief, is pointed. The grammatical-historical voices note the evangelist's reserve: Ellicott and Robertson observe that the other disciple is left unnamed because he is John himself, who "will even here draw the veil over his own identity," while Andrew is identified by his relation to the better-known Peter. Ellicott reads the first day as "the earnest of the harvest to come," yielding two pairs of brothers — the sons of Zebedee and the sons of Jonas — "who are ever leaders in the apostolic band." Robertson adds that the more formal call of these men to apostleship comes only later, by the Sea of Galilee (Mark 1:16).

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a study in the nature of faith, which "does not conceal or quench the light, but rather spreads it in every direction." Andrew, with scarcely a spark, at once enlightens his brother, fulfilling the double duty Isaiah requires of God's children — to take one's neighbour by the hand and say, "Come, let us go up... and he will teach us" (Isaiah 2:3). Calvin marks God's purpose that Peter, "who was to be far more eminent," should be brought to Christ "by the agency and ministry of Andrew," so that none, however excellent, should disdain to be taught by an inferior; the proud man "will be severely punished," he warns, who through contempt of a lesser brother will not come to Christ. "Woe to our indolence," he concludes, if having been enlightened we do not seek to make others partakers of the same grace. Gill marks that Andrew, not Peter, was the first of the apostles to come to Christ; and Robertson recalls that the more formal call of these brothers to apostleship comes only later, by the sea (Mark 1:16), so that this verse records not the call but the first acquaintance and conviction. Ellicott reads this first day as "the earnest of the harvest to come," yielding two pairs of brothers, the sons of Zebedee and the sons of Jonas, "who are ever leaders in the apostolic band," each disciple in the joy of his own discovery going to seek his own brother.
41:
Andrew "first findeth his own brother Simon" with the news, "We have found the Messias." The witnesses agree on the joy and communicativeness of faith, and on the meaning of the title. The Latin fathers expound the name: Augustine glosses Messias / Christus / Unctus, the Anointed whose chrism overflows from him to all Christians, he himself being "the Holy of Holies, specially anointed... above His fellows" (Psalm 45:7); Bede presses on to the name-change, Peter from petra, the rock that is Christ — though the formal naming waits for Matthew 16:18. Chrysostom pictures Andrew running "in haste" to report the good tidings, the cry "We have found" expressing "a longing for His coming, turned to exultation," and commends Peter's ready obedience in going at once.

The grammatical-historical voices weigh a textual point: does Andrew find his brother "first" (proton) — implying John afterward found his own brother James — or is Andrew "the first" (protos) to do so (Robertson, Ellicott)? Either way, Ellicott reads the verse as quietly implying that John too sought out James, in the reserve characteristic of this Gospel; Lightfoot notes the Talmudic idiom "We have found!" They observe that the Hebrew form "Messias" appears in the New Testament only here and at John 4:25, both vivid remembered scenes, elsewhere rendered by the Greek "Christ." The Reformed Calvin traces the title's force to Daniel 9:25, where, unlike the earlier prophets who used it of kings, it is appropriated to the Redeemer alone; and he marvels that one so universally awaited "should be received by so small a number of persons." The Wesleyan voices (Wesley, Clarke) read the same impulse pastorally: grace is communicative, each new convert seeks first his own household, and "those who have been brought to an acquaintance with God" must endeavour to bring another with them. The Alexandrian Origen stands apart, harmonising this finding with the later call by the Sea of Galilee (Matthew 4:18). Bede grounds the new name in "the firmness of his faith, in cleaving to that Rock" which is Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4), while Augustine holds that God "made him to be called by another name before, that the change of that name might give vividness to the mystery."
42:
Andrew brings Simon to Jesus, who looks on him and says, "Thou art Simon... thou shalt be called Cephas, which is by interpretation, A stone." The grammatical-historical voices handle the names: Cephas is Aramaic (rather Chaldee/Syriac), rendered by the Greek Peter, a stone (Barnes, Clarke); Lightfoot insists the evangelist gives the Greek proper name answering the Syriac, not a lexicon-gloss, as "Tabitha" is rendered "Dorcas" (Acts 9:36). Robertson notes the future passive "thou shalt be called" as prophecy spoken apparently before Simon even spoke, and recalls the classical distinction between petra (the massive ledge) and petros (a detached stone), while granting that the underlying Aramaic would not mark it. Barnes finds in Christ's naming a proof of his omniscience: foreseeing that Simon, "rash, headlong, variable" before the resurrection, would become a man of rock-like firmness, and playing on Jonas ("a dove") against the new name of constancy.

The Reformed Calvin reads the name prophetically — given "not as men commonly do, from some past event," but because Christ "was to make him Peter" — and turns it into a polemic against Rome. Peter, he insists, is himself "founded on Christ along with the rest of the disciples," not made the foundation or head of the church; and he charges the canonists (the spurious canon of Anacletus in Gratian) with confusing the Greek kephale, "head," with the Aramaic cephas, "stone." The name, for Calvin, magnifies the grace that would make a man of obscure birth into one of unshaken courage, "a new man." The Catena fathers, by contrast, read the same naming as Christ's prerogative — as he renamed Abram and Jacob — and as anchoring Peter on the Rock that is Christ, anticipating Matthew 16:18. Lightfoot defends the spelling "Jona" as a good Hebrew name in its own right, not a contraction of "Joannes"; and Calvin presses his polemic to its sharpest, that Rome "doubly" errs — first setting Peter in Christ's place as the church's foundation, then "out of a stone" making him a head, confounding the Aramaic Cephas with the Greek kephale.
43:
Jesus, going forth into Galilee, finds Philip and says, "Follow me." The witnesses agree the wonder of the verse is the bare efficacy of that word. The Antiochene Chrysostom presses the contrast: Andrew had heard from John and Peter from Andrew, but Philip "learned nothing from any but Christ," and yet at the single word "Follow me" obeyed instantly and became himself a preacher — for the voice of Christ, says Theophylact, "kindled in their inmost soul the love of Him." Chrysostom adds that Christ called none until some had come of their own free choice, so that, "having chosen this of themselves, they afterwards remained firm"; and that, though the Galileans were reckoned "of a more boorish and dull disposition," Christ displayed his power "by selecting from a land which bore no fruit His choicest disciples," choosing the weak things of the world (1 Corinthians 1:27). The Latin Alcuin reads the names mystically — Bethsaida "house of hunters," fishers of souls; Galilee a place of "transition" or "revelation" — and "Follow me" as a call to imitate Christ's humility and suffering, that we may share his resurrection.

The Reformed Calvin makes this the text for the power of the gospel word: Philip was "inflamed by this single word to follow Christ," yet the same word strikes many ears in vain "just as if he struck their ears with a sound which vanished into air." The external preaching, he says, is "in itself unfruitful... except the secret grace of God quicken it," for which "we ought to pray to Christ." The Puritan Matthew Henry adds the note of preventing grace — "Jesus findeth Philip"; Christ "sought us, and found us, before we made any enquiries after him" — and the diligence that when "work is to be done for God, we must not lose a day."

The grammatical-historical voices harmonise the chronology: Barnes carefully distinguishes this acquaintance-and-conviction from the later formal call to apostleship at the sea (Matthew 4:18), so that John and the Synoptics do not conflict; Robertson and Ellicott note that "Follow me" is the very verb by which Jesus repeatedly wins disciples, used outside the Twelve only of the rich young man (Matthew 19:21).
44:
The note that "Philip was of Bethsaida, the city of Andrew and Peter" draws brief but pointed comment. The grammatical-historical voices treat it as one of the eye-witness touches of local knowledge that give this Gospel its colour. This is Bethsaida of Galilee (Ellicott, Robertson, cf. John 12:21), distinct from the Bethsaida Julias east of the lake in Iturea; Ellicott notes it "explains the meeting," Philip being on his way home along the road Jesus would take from Bethania to Cana (John 2:1), and explains too the process by which a fellow-townsman of Andrew and Peter passed from Messianic hope to full belief. Robertson distinguishes the two Bethsaidas — the eastern Julias and the western town of Galilee near Capernaum — and concludes that Philip "would be inclined to follow the example of his townsmen." Barnes adds only that this was "the city of Andrew and Peter."

The Reformed Calvin alone draws doctrine from the place: its very name is mentioned, he thinks, "that the goodness of God to the three Apostles may be more illustriously displayed," for Christ elsewhere severely curses Bethsaida (Matthew 11:21; Luke 10:13). To draw apostles from so ungodly a town, and to honour them so highly as to make them apostles, Calvin says, should be viewed "as if they had been brought out of the lowest hell" — a distinguished favour worth recording. The Reformed Jamieson-Fausset-Brown add only Bengel's terse aside that the work was "not done without dust on man's part" — the human means by which grace travels from neighbour to neighbour. Gill adds the local colour that Bethsaida, "a fishing town" on the lake of Gennesaret, was afterward enlarged by Philip the tetrarch and renamed Julias after Caesar's daughter, its very name fitting the trade of the disciples drawn from it; so that, as Calvin urges, the naming both authenticates the scene and magnifies the grace that called fishermen from an unbelieving town. Robertson distinguishes the two towns of the name — the eastern Bethsaida Julias in Iturea and this western Bethsaida of Galilee near Capernaum — concluding that Philip, a townsman of Andrew and Peter, "would be inclined to follow the example of his townsmen."
45:
Philip finds Nathanael with the news, "We have found him of whom Moses in the law and the prophets did write, Jesus of Nazareth, the son of Joseph." The witnesses agree on the substance — that Philip announces the fulfilment of Deuteronomy 18:18, Genesis 49:10, and the prophets — and most identify Nathanael with Bartholomew (Clarke, Wesley, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott), since the Synoptics pair Bartholomew with Philip while John never names him, and John ranks Nathanael among the apostles at John 21:2. The grammatical-historical voices note Philip's diligent searching of the Scriptures and read "son of Joseph... of Nazareth" as the common supposition of the day; Ellicott (observing that Nathanael is the Hebrew of "Theodorus," God's gift) prizes the evangelist's "dramatic accuracy" in giving the words exactly as spoken, without correcting them by his own knowledge of the birth at Bethlehem (cf. John 7:52), and Robertson, recalling that John has just called Jesus "God-only begotten" (v.18), speaks of "the irony of St. John."

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a lesson on faulty-but-sincere witness: Philip errs twice — calling Christ "son of Joseph" and a native of Nazareth — "and yet, because he is sincerely desirous to do good to his brother, and to make Christ known, God approves" his diligence and crowns it with success, since his witness, however involved in error, "had this for its object, that Christ might be truly known." Better, Calvin urges, to "stammer ridiculously... and to hold by the true Christ, than by eloquent and ingenious language to introduce a false Christ" — a thrust at the Papists, who "annihilate his power, so as to hold out a phantom in the room of Christ." The chief design of all doctrine, he concludes, is that those who hear "should come to Christ in some way or other." The Wesleyan Wesley likewise notes that Philip thought him of Nazareth, "not knowing he was born in Bethlehem." Robertson catches the momentum — "Philip carries on the work. One wins one" — and Gill places Nathanael at Cana of Galilee, whither Jesus was even then going, so that the chain of witness runs unbroken from John to Andrew to Peter to Philip and now to Nathanael.
46:
Nathanael's "Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?" and Philip's "Come and see" draw a shared reading: prejudice met by invitation to examine. The grammatical-historical voices explain the scorn — Nazareth's poor repute, perhaps sharpened by town-rivalry, Cana (Nathanael's home) being near (Robertson, Ellicott) — and note the parallel slur, "out of Galilee ariseth no prophet" (John 7:52), which Robertson calls "untrue, like many such sayings." Clarke, following Pearce, suggests Nathanael may rather mean that the Messiah, the promised "good thing," was looked for from Bethlehem, not Nazareth (Jeremiah 33:15). Barnes turns it into a maxim against pre-judging: many "suffer their minds to be filled with prejudice against religion, and then pronounce at once without examination"; the cure is not to reason but to "come and see," to make trial for oneself, and the "petty and foolish objections" will vanish.

The Reformed Calvin diagnoses a twofold error: Nathanael too readily believes Philip's misstatement, and is swayed by contempt of the place — both being Satan's stratagems to keep men from Christ, "to make us look on Christ with contempt," whether by spreading falsehood or by the offence of his lowliness, "the degradation of the cross, which appears both in Christ the head and in his members." Yet Calvin commends Nathanael's readiness to let his prejudice be corrected by inquiry, and rejects as "greatly mistaken" the reading that takes the words not as a question but as an affirmation. The Wesleyan Wesley draws the same lesson: "how cautiously should we guard against popular prejudices," which led even so honest a heart to suspect Christ an impostor, until "his integrity prevailed over that foolish bias, and laid him open to the force of evidence." Clarke widens "Come and see" into a charge against unbelief itself: "he who candidly examines the evidences of the religion of Christ will infallibly become a believer," for none should judge "by first appearances, or human prejudices." Robertson commends Philip's method — not argument but invitation — as the wisdom that "knows how to deal with the sceptic," while Lightfoot notes how common in the rabbis was the very phrase "Come and see."
47:
"Behold an Israelite indeed, in whom is no guile" sets the witnesses on the figure of Jacob/Israel. With broad agreement they hear an allusion to the patriarch: the grammatical-historical voices note that the word for "guile" (dolos) is the very term the Greek Old Testament uses of the subtlety by which Jacob took the blessing (Genesis 27:35), so that Nathanael is an Israel without the supplanter's guile, like the Servant in whose mouth was no deceit (Isaiah 53:9). Lightfoot pictures him under the fig tree in private prayer — an "Israelite indeed" precisely because, unlike the street-corner hypocrites who "pray... that they might be seen of men," he sought retirement to pray, "so different from the usual craft and hypocrisy of that nation." Ellicott presses the parallel of Jacob "left alone" and wrestling, prevailing with God and seeing him "face to face" (Genesis 32:24).

The Latin Augustine guards the praise from misunderstanding: "no guile" does not mean "no sin," for none is born without need of the Physician; guile is "when we say one thing, and think another," and Nathanael lacks it because, being a sinner, he confesses his sin rather than feigning righteousness — Christ "commended the confession of sin in Nathanael; He did not pronounce him not a sinner" (Psalm 32:2). The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a definition of true Christianity — "integrity of heart before God, and uprightness before men" — set against the hypocrisy that names itself Israel; for "they are not all Israel who are of Israel" (Romans 9:6), and Christ here forewarns of the nation's coming unbelief, that none be discouraged when those who claim descent from Abraham become "the deadly enemies of the Gospel." The Antiochene Chrysostom adds that Nathanael's very hesitation showed him guileless — speaking "nothing to gain favour, or gratify malice" — and that he was "more deeply read in the Prophets than Philip." Barnes presses the same definition from the other side: Nathanael "makes no profession which he does not live up to," a man with "no disguise, no trick, no hypocrisy" — though Barnes is careful that this means not sinless but sincere, a testimony one could wish the Searcher of hearts might bear of all who profess the gospel.
48:
"Whence knowest thou me? ... Before that Philip called thee, when thou wast under the fig tree, I saw thee." The witnesses are united that Christ here displays a divine, heart-searching sight. The grammatical-historical voices fill in the setting: the fig tree was a customary place of private prayer and meditation, the Jewish rabbins choosing "the shade of trees, and particularly the fig tree, to sit and study under" (Clarke); Jesus saw not merely Nathanael's body but his secret heart and worship, "I saw thee" meaning his thoughts and desires were open (Robertson). Barnes draws out the lessons in order: that Jesus "sees what is done in secret, and is therefore divine"; that "he sees us when we little think of it"; that "he sees us especially in our private devotions"; and that "he judges of our character chiefly by our private devotions" — whence it becomes us that our secret prayers be "without guile and hypocrisy" (Psalm 139:2). Ellicott pictures the fig tree of Nathanael's own garden, the place where, "not at the corners of the streets, or to be seen of men," he prayed unseen by any but Him to whose coming every true Israelite looked (Micah 4:4; Zechariah 3:10).

The Reformed Calvin presses the inference to its point: both to know hearts and to see what is hidden from the eye "belongs to God alone," and from things of the same class Nathanael rightly concludes that Christ does not now "speak as a man." The useful doctrine, Calvin adds, is "that when we are not thinking of Christ, we are observed by him; and it is necessary that it should be so, that he may bring us back, when we have wandered from the right path." Clarke turns it to comfort and warning alike: it is "the comfort of the sincere... that God knows their hearts," and "the terror of... the hypocrite, that their false dealing is ever noticed" — "Thou God seest me!" Gill catches Nathanael's astonishment that one "a stranger to him" should "describe the internal state and frame of his soul"; and Clarke notes the article — "not A fig tree, but THE fig tree" — the particular, familiar place of his retreat, where he supposed no eye but God's could reach him.
49:
Nathanael's confession — "Rabbi, thou art the Son of God; thou art the King of Israel" — opens a genuine debate over its depth. The Antiochene Chrysostom raises it pointedly: Nathanael and Peter (Matthew 16:16) speak "the same words, but not... with the same intention." Peter confessed "Son of God" as very God, and was called blessed; Nathanael, Chrysostom argues, meant it of a mere man, "for after saying, Thou art the Son of God, he adds, Thou art the King of Israel" — a narrower title, since the Son of God is King "not of Israel only, but of the whole world" — and so, his confession being "very deficient," he is "led up to higher things." The Reformed Calvin reads the confession as real but limited: faith "ought not to be fixed on the essence of Christ alone... but ought to attend to his power and office" as King; yet Nathanael's "King of Israel" is still "limited to the measure of faith," confined within narrow bounds, "not yet" the world-wide kingdom which we, to whom its extent is revealed, must confess. The Papists, Calvin adds, err the opposite way, gazing at Christ's mere essence while "they annihilate his power."

The grammatical-historical voices resist toning the confession down. Robertson holds there is "no reason for toning down the noble confession of Nathanael," both being Messianic titles drawn from Psalm 2:7, "King of Israel" reappearing at the Triumphal Entry (John 12:13) and over the cross (John 19:19), and comparing the like confessions of Peter (John 6:69) and Martha (John 11:27). Barnes reasons that Nathanael, finding his heart read, concluded "he could not be a mere man, but must be the long-expected Messiah." Wesley reads it plainly as "a confession both of the person and office of Christ." Thus the schools divide: the Greek father and the Reformer hear a true but incipient faith to be enlarged, while the grammarians hear a full and noble acknowledgment. Wesley reads the swiftness as blessing — "happy are they that are ready to believe, swift to receive the truth and grace of God" — and Gill grounds the confession simply in Nathanael's being "fully convinced of his omniscience by these instances."
50:
"Because I said... I saw thee under the fig tree, believest thou? thou shalt see greater things than these." The comment is brief and harmonious. The grammatical-historical voices note that "believest thou?" may be read as a question or, better, as an assertion — "on this evidence thou believest" — Ellicott comparing the same phrase at John 16:31 and John 20:29, and adding that "the use of the faith-faculty strengthens it." The "greater things," they agree, are the fuller proofs of his Messiahship promised in v.51 (Barnes, Clarke). Clarke draws the encouragement that Nathanael's faith, resting now on a single sign, "shall not rest merely upon this," but see "more numerous and express proofs" of Christ's "eternal power and Godhead." Robertson takes the future as possibly volitive — "thou shalt see" — and observes that "the wonder of Nathanael no doubt grew as Jesus went on."

The Reformed Calvin reads it not as a reproof of over-easy belief but as Christ's approval of a ready faith: "he does not reprove Nathanael as if he had been too easy of belief, but rather approving of his faith, promises to him and to others that he will confirm it by stronger arguments." And he marks the shift from the private token given to one man toward a proof that would be "common to all" — Christ "as if he had broken off from what he originally intended," turning from addressing one man to address them all, which leads directly into the great promise of the next verse. Clarke paraphrases the promise warmly: because Nathanael "credited my Divine mission on this simple proof," his faith "shall not rest merely upon this," but shall behold "more numerous and express proofs of my eternal power and Godhead." Barnes fixes the "greater things" on the vision of the next verse; Gill hears in Christ's answer not reproof but surprise and approval at so ready a faith; and Calvin, marking the turn from the sign given to one man to a proof "common to all," sees Christ already widening his address from Nathanael to the whole company of disciples.
51:
Christ's solemn promise — "Verily, verily... ye shall see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of man" — finds the witnesses unanimous on its root: this is the fulfilment of Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12), with Christ himself the bond between heaven and earth. The grammatical-historical voices note the doubled "Amen," found in the New Testament only on Christ's lips in this Gospel — he is himself "the Amen, the faithful witness" (Revelation 3:14; so Lightfoot, from Isaiah 65:16) — and the plural "ye shall see," addressed beyond Nathanael to all the disciples. The perfect participle pictures heaven "standing open," the symbol of free intercourse between God and man (Isaiah 64:1), later realised in Stephen's vision (Acts 7:56). Robertson and Ellicott press the title "Son of man": over against Nathanael's Jewish titles, it names Christ as the true representative of the race — homo, not vir — the second Adam in whom heaven and earth meet. Clarke reads the angels as couriers between the prince and his ambassador, the whole work of reconciliation now passing through the Son of man (2 Corinthians 5:19).

The Reformed Calvin warns against fixing the promise on one time or place: it points to "something perpetual," for the kingdom of God, once closed against us, is now opened in Christ; the angels ascend and descend as ministers of God's kindness to the whole Church, for Christ's honour — shown visibly to Stephen (Acts 7:55), on the mount (Matthew 17:5), and at the Ascension (Acts 1:9). The Wesleyan Wesley, with Barnes, leans rather to particular future events — "ye shall see me come in my glory with all the angels," or the ministrations of angels at the resurrection and ascension. So the schools agree on Jacob's ladder and the Son of man, and divide only on whether the "hereafter" is a perpetual mediatorial state (Calvin, Ellicott) or a series of coming events (Wesley, Barnes). The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem touches the verse only to gather from the doubled "verily" the promise of life eternal by faith.
